---
title: Growing heirloom tomatoes
date: 2024-05-11
tags: [gardening, cooking, tomatoes]
---

I start seeds under lights in February. Brandywine, Cherokee Purple, and Green Zebra are my reliable favorites, and I trial one or two new varieties each year in a small corner of the bed. Indeterminate varieties take most of the space because they keep producing until frost.

Deep planting is the trick for strong tomatoes. I bury the stem up to the first set of true leaves, which sprouts roots all along the buried portion and gives the plant a much larger anchor. A tall wooden stake goes in at the same time so I never disturb the roots later.

Consistent water is what separates a good crop from a cracked one. I mulch heavily with straw, water deeply twice a week instead of lightly every day, and prune suckers to keep air moving through the canopy. The reward is a paste of ripe fruit that puts any store tomato to shame.
